content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660          322-5901          322-6441            322-6444

July 26, 1983


Robert M. Myers
City Attorney
1685 Main Street
Santa Monica, CA  90401

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice on
                                             Behalf of James Conn,
                                             Our Advice No. A-83-164

Dear Mr. Myers:

     Your request for written advice on behalf of Councilmember
James Conn presents the following facts for our consideration.

                                    FACTS

     1.  James Conn is a member of the City Council of the City
of Santa Monica.

     2.  Councilmember James Conn is employed as a minister at
The Church in Ocean Park (United Methodist).  Appointment of a
minister at a particular United Methodist Church is made by the
United Methodist Church (Annual Conference).  The Administrative
Board of The Church in Ocean Park does not employ nor does it
terminate a minister since those decisions are made at the
discretion of the presiding Bishop.

     3.  The Church in Ocean Park owns certain real property in
the City of Santa Monica.  The real property, which consists of
a number of contiguous lots, is improved with three structures.
One of the structures consists of a church facility.  The
remaining structures consist of single family structures.  The
two single family structures are not needed for day-to-day
church operations and have been rented to non-profit community
organizations.

     4.  One of the organizations that rents church property is
the Ocean Park Community Organizations (OPCO).  OPCO has rented
space at The Church since February 1, 1980.  Beginning with
January 1983, the rent was $700 per month.  The rent includes

Robert M. Myers
July 26, 1983
Page 2


the lease of one of the two single family structures and the
right to use from time to time certain facilities of the main
church facility.

     5.  The governing board of The Church has developed over
time a basic rental/use policy.  The policy is as follows:

     a)  The basic purpose and goals of the agency must be
compatible with the principles of The Church in Ocean Park
(United Methodist) and the United Methodist Church.

     b)  The agency must be a non-profit 501(c)(3)
organization.

     c)  The agency's rent must be negotiated and approved
by the Administrative Board of The Church or approved upon
adoption of the annual budget of The Church by the Charge
Conference of The Church.

     d)  The agency must accept responsibility for basic
maintenance; larger maintenance efforts are negotiated.

     e)  The agency must carry its own basic liability
insurance.

     6.  Rental rates and the salary of the minister are set at
the annual meeting of the Administrative Board of the congre-
gation of The Church in Ocean Park.  While procedurally the
salary is set by motion at the Charge Conference, Rev. Conn's
salary was actually set by the Annual Conference.

     The local church through the Administrative Board is
responsible for meeting The Church's fiscal needs, including
compensation of the minister.  If The Church cannot meet its
fiscal obligations, the Annual Conference can add salary
support, but no such supplement has been received since only
1976.  The 1983 Annual Budget for The Church in Ocean Park is
$70,668 with income of $70,680.

     7.  OPCO is a non-profit corporation formed on approximately
April 26, 1978.  The current director of OPCO, Judy Abdo, and
Councilmember Conn were among the original directors of the non-
profit corporation, but Rev. Conn has not been a director for
over two years.  (When he served as a director, Councilmember
Conn received no compensation for his services.)  The Articles
of Incorporation provide for distribution of OPCO's assets to
The Church in Ocean Park or some other 501(c)(3) tax-exempt
organization should it dissolve.

Robert M. Myers
July 26, 1983
Page 3


8.  OPCO has been the recipient of grants from the City of
Santa Monica to perform certain specified activities.  These
grants are administered through a contract entered into between
the City of Santa Monica and OPCO.  The source of grant funds
are Community Development Block Grant Funds and General Revenue
Sharing Funds.  Last year these grants represented approximately
95% of OPCO's budget.

     OPCO has indicated that even if it lost the funding which it
receives from the City, it would continue to rent the premises,
although it might sublease a portion of the premises if it were
forced to cut back on its operations.  However, since much of
the City funds went for materials, a loss of the City contract
would not result in a proportionate reduction in OPCO staffing.
Ms. Abdo believes that replacement funding could be secured
through fundraising.

     9.  The fair rental value of the premises rented by OPCO
from The Church is in excess of $700 per month.  If OPCO vacates
the premises, others have already indicated their interest in
taking it over.

                              QUESTIONS

     You have posed the following questions.

     1.  May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or
in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that
OPCO rents property from The Church in Ocean Park?

     2.  May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or
in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that
the Articles of Incorporation of OPCO provide that upon its
dissolution its assets may be given to The Church in Ocean Park?

     3.  If OPCO lawfully terminates its tenancy with The Church,
at what point in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision concerning OPCO?

     4.  If OPCO amends its articles of incorporation to
specifically designate some nonprofit organization other than
The Church to receive its assets upon dissolution, at what point
in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision concerning OPCO?

Robert M. Myers
July 26, 1983
Page 4


ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act/ dictates that a public official
may not make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence any official decision in which he or she
has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87100.

A financial interest in a decision exists whenever the
reasonably foreseeable effect of that decision will be both
material and distinguishable (from the decision's effect upon
the public generally) on:

(a) Any business entity in which the official has an
investment of more than $1,000;

(b) Any real property in which the official has an
interest of more than $1,000;

(c) Any source of income to the official of $250 or
more during the preceding 12-month period; or

(d) Any business entity in which the official is a
partner, officer, employee, etc.

Section 87103.

In the case of Councilmember Conn, The Church in Ocean Park
is not a business entity (Section 82005). Therefore neither
Section 87103(a) or (d) applies. Rev. Conn does not have an
ownership interest in the real property owned by The Church.
Section 87103(b). However, The Church is a source of income to
Rev. Conn of $250 or more during the last 12 months. OPCO is
not a source of income to Rev. Conn and he has no other
financial interest in it.

Consequently, the only basis for requiring disqualification
is if the decision on the OPCO contract will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on The Church. The income
from the space rented by OPCO constitutes approximately 12% of
The Church's annual gross revenues. Clearly, loss of this
income would constitute a material financial effect upon The
Church. However, such loss does not appear to be foreseeable

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

Robert M. Myers
July 26, 1983
Page 5


because we are told that OPCO will continue to rent the space
and sublease, if necessary. Even if OPCO did vacate the
premises, we are advised that others are waiting to step in and
rent the premises at the current rate.

Based upon these facts2/ we must conclude that fore-
seability is lacking and, therefore, disqualification is not
required. This is true both as to the rental issue and the
dissolution issue, since it is apparent that OPCO will not
dissolve and will continue to rent. Given this conclusion, we
need not address your third and fourth questions.

Should you have any questions regarding this advice, please
do not hesitate to call me at 916/322-5901.

Sincerely,


Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

REL:km


2/ The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when
it issues legal advice. Our advice is applicable only to the
extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all
the material facts have been provided. Oglesby Opinion, 1 FPPC
Opinions 71, 77 fn. 6, No. 75-083, July 2, 1975.

CITY OF

SANTA MONICA
CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
1685 Main Street
Santa Monica, California 90401
Telephone: (213) 393-9975

July 5, 1983

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Department
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Written Advice

Gentlepeople:

Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b), this
office, on behalf of Councilmember James Conn, requests
written advice on certain questions based upon the following
material facts.

MATERIAL FACTS

1. James Conn is an elected member of the City Council
of the City of Santa Monica. He was elected to a four year
term on the City Council during the April 1981 General
Municipal Election.

2. Councilmember James Conn is employed as a minister
at The Church in Ocean Park (United Methodist). Appointment
of a minister at a particular United Methodist Church is made
by the Bishop of the Pacific and Southwest Annual Conference
of the United Methodist Church (Annual Conference). Rev. Conn
serves one year appointments and has been reappointed annually
since July 1973 by the presiding Bishop. The Administrative
Board of The Church in Ocean Park does not employ nor does it
terminate a minister since those decisions are made at the
discretion of the presiding Bishop.

Page 2
Fair Political Practices Commission
July 5, 1983


3.  The Church in Ocean Park owns certain real property
in the City of Santa Monica.  The real property, which
consists of a number of contiguous lots, is improved with
three structures.  One of the structures consists of a Church
facility.  The remaining structures consists of single family
structures.  The two single family structures are not needed
for day-to-day church operations and have been rented to
non-profit community organizations.

4.  One of the organizations that rents church property
is the Ocean Park Community Organization (OPCO).  OPCO, and
its predecessor Ocean Park Projects/Communitas, has rented
space at the church since February 1, 1980.  Beginning with
January, 1983, the rent was $700.00 per month.  The rent
includes the lease of one of the two single family structures
and the right to use from time to time certain facilities of
the main church facility.

5.  The governing board of the church has developed over
time a basic rental/use policy.  The policy is as follows:

     a.  The basic purpose and goals of the agency must be
compatible with the principles of the Church in Ocean Park
(United Methodist) and the United Methodist Church.

     b.  The agency must be a non-profit 501(c)(3)
organization.

     c.  The agency's rent must be negotiated and approved by
the Administrative Board of the Church or approved
upon adoption of the annual budget of the Church by
the Charge Conference of the Church.

     d.  The agency must accept responsibility for basic
maintenance; larger maintenance efforts are
negotiated.

     e.  The agency must carry its own basic liability
insurance.

6.  Rental rates and the salary of the minister is set
at the annual meeting of the Charge Conference.  The Charge
Conference is a meeting of the Administrative Board of the
congregation of the Church in Ocean Park.  The meeting is set
and chaired by the District Superintendent of the United
Methodist Church.  Rev. Conn's annual salary is $22,232.00.
While procedurally the salary is set by motion at the Charge
Conference, Rev. Conn's salary was actually set by the Annual
Conference.

Page 3
Fair Political Practices Commission
July 5, 1983

     The Annual Conference has set a minimum salary for all
United Methodist ministers serving in the Annual Conference of
a figure not less than 80% of the average Conference
Minister's salary.  Since his appointment by the presiding
Bishop in July 1973, Rev. Conn has always received the minimum
salary permitted.  The local church through the Administrative
Board is responsible for meeting the church's fiscal needs,
including compensation of the minister.  If the church cannot
meet its fiscal obligations, the Annual Conference can add
salary support, but no such supplement has been received since
July, 1976.  The 1983 Annual Budget for the Church in Ocean
Park is $70,668.00 with income of $70,680.00.

     Councilmember Conn receives no other remuneration than
his annual salary, which has always been the minimum required
by the Annual Conference.

     7.  OPCO is a non-profit corporation formed on
approximately April 26, 1978.  The current director of OPCO,
Judy Abdo, and Councilmember Conn were among the original
directors of the non-profit corporation, but Rev. Conn has not
been a director for over two years.  (When he served as a
director, Councilmember Conn received no compensation for his
services.)  The Articles of Incorporation provide for
distribution of OPCO's assets to the Church in Ocean Park
should it dissolve.  In addition, Ms. Abdo has served as
President of the Church in Ocean Park in the past and is
currently performing some office work for the church such as
bookkeeping.

     8.  OPCO has been the recipient of grants from the City
of Santa Monica to perform certain specified activities.
These grants are administered through a contract entered into
between the City of Santa Monica and OPCO.  The source of
grant funds are Community Development Block Grant Funds and
General Revenue Sharing Funds.

     9.  The fair rental value of the premises rented by OPCO
from the Church is in excess of $700 per month.

                    REQUEST FOR WRITTEN ADVICE

     Based upon the foregoing facts, Councilmember James Conn
requests written advice on the following questions:

     1.  May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making,
or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of
the fact that OPCO rents property from the Church in Ocean
Park.

Page 4
Fair Political Practices Commission
July 5, 1983

2. May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that the Articles of Incorporation of OPCO provide that upon its dissolution its assets shall be given to the Church in Ocean Park.

3. If the OPCO lawfully terminates its tenancy with the Church, at what point in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision concerning OPCO.

4. If OPCO amends its articles of incorporation to designate some other nonprofit organization other than the Church to receive its assets upon dissolution, at what point in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision concerning OPCO.

If you need any additional information in order to respond to this question, please do not hesitate to contact this office.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]
ROBERT M. MYERS
City Attorney

RMM:vc

cc: Councilmember James Conn

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

July 8, 1983

Robert M. Myers
City Attorney
1685 Main Street
Santa Monica, CA 90401
Re: A-83-164

Dear Mr. Myers:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert E. Leidigh, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilmember James Conn, who is employed as a minister at The Church in Ocean Park which receives rent from OPCO, must disqualify himself from participating in City decisions concerning OPCO's grants and contracts.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Councilmember Conn is not required to disqualify himself from OPCO decisions because while The Church is a source of income to him, loss of OPCO's rental payments is not reasonably foreseeable given that OPCO will continue renting and alternative tenants are available.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (July 5, 1983) and FPPC response (July 26, 1983). Extraction based on the FPPC response letter. Footnote clarifies Commission does not act as fact-finder and advice is conditional on accuracy of provided facts.

governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that 
OPCO rents property from The Church in Ocean Park?

Robert M. Myers 
July 26, 1983

This is true both as to the rental issue and the dissolution issue, since it is apparent that OPCO will not dissolve and will continue to rent. Given this conclusion, we need not address your third and fourth questions.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice on whether Councilmember James Conn must disqualify himself from City decisions affecting OPCO, a nonprofit that rents property from The Church where Conn is employed as a minister. The FPPC concluded disqualification is not required because loss of rental income from OPCO is not reasonably foreseeable.
